description: 'munari' is loose particulate material moved or deposited by water, wind, ice, or gravity. Other processes can move it as well. Sediment may contain mineral, organic, or mixed matter. 'muila' names earth in general; erosion needs evidence of detachment and movement, while pollution needs evidence of impairment.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for 'm' and continue the voiced nasal into high-back rounded 'u'. Place the tongue tip against the upper teeth for dental 'n' and open into stressed central 'a'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip, then finish on high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The rounded enclosure of 'mu' opens broadly through stressed dental 'na', and final 'ri' leaves one small turn of the tongue. The heavier beginning seems to settle while the ending keeps a trace of movement.
usage_notes: 'munari' has both count and mass uses. Grain size, composition, and amount stay in their source records when precision matters. Sample identity and method remain there too. 'wheraku' names erosion; 'muila', 'muhena', and 'kirua' name earth, dust, and sand.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Sediment may be habitat material, transported matter, or a burden. Its place and effect decide which account fits.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: River color, deposited silt, shifting banks, and settled particles have long been practical signs of land and water change.

semantic_domains.nature: particulate material moved or deposited by environmental processes
semantic_domains.physical: loose mineral, organic, or mixed particles
